div.p: What promises are made in relation to the subject of the salvation of the dead?1 and what kind of characters are those who can be saved, although their bodies are mouldering and decaying in the grave? When his commandments teach us, it is in view of eternity; for we are looked upon by God2 as though we were in eternity. God dwells in eternity,3 and does not view things as we do.4

The greatest responsibility in this world that God has laid upon us is to seek after our dead. The Apostle says, “They without us cannot be made perfect;”5 (Hebrews 11:40)6 for it is necessary that the sealing power7 should be in our hands to seal our children and our dead8 for the fulness of the dispensation of times9—a dispensation to meet the promises made by Jesus Christ before the foundation of the world10 for the salvation of man.11

Now, I will speak of them. I will meet Paul half way. I say to you, Paul, you cannot be perfect without us.12 It is necessary that those who are going before and those who come after us should have salvation in common13 with us; and thus hath God made it obligatory upon man. Hence, God said, “I will send you Elijah the prophet before the coming of the great and dreadful day of the Lord; and he shall turn the heart of the fathers to the children, and the heart of the children to their fathers, lest I come and smite the earth with a curse.” (Malachi 4:5–6.)14

I have a declaration to make as to the provisions which God hath made to suit the conditions of man15—made from before the foundation of the world.16 What has Jesus said? All sin, and all blasphemies, and every transgression, except one, that man can be guilty of, may be forgiven;17 and there is a salvation for all men, either in this world or the world to come,18 who have not committed the unpardonable sin,19 there being a pro-

tion of his being a fallen prophet could be made. The Prophet lived his life in crescendo. From small beginnings, it rose in breadth and power as he neared its close. As a teacher he reached the climax of his career in this discourse. After it there was but one thing more he could do—seal his testimony with his blood. This he did less than three months later. Such is not the manner of life of false prophets.—Note by Elder B. H. Roberts.
